content: Mordecai said to Esther: 'Who knows whether you have come to the kingdom for ___?'
explanation: Esther 4:14 — Mordecai believed God placed Esther on the throne at the very moment the Jewish people needed deliverance.

content: Why was Queen Vashti deposed?
explanation: Esther 1:12 — Queen Vashti refused to come before the king. This opened the way for Esther to become queen.
options: Refused to come when summoned at the wine feast

content: Of what people was Esther?
explanation: Esther 2:5-7 — Her Jewish name was Hadassah; Esther is her Persian name. Orphaned, raised by Mordecai.

content: Esther was chosen as queen in place of whom?
explanation: Esther 2:17 — The king loved Esther more than all the others and set the royal crown on her head.

content: Why did Haman want to destroy the Jewish people?
explanation: Esther 3:5-6 — Mordecai refused to bow to Haman. Haman's pride drove him to seek the destruction of an entire people.
options: Because Mordecai refused to bow to him
Because the Jews paid no taxes

content: By casting Pur (the lot), what day did Haman choose to destroy the Jews?
explanation: Esther 3:7,13 — The word 'Purim' (lots) became the name of the festival commemorating the deliverance.

content: What did Esther ask the people to do before she went to the king uninvited?
explanation: Esther 4:16 — Esther was ready to die: 'If I perish, I perish.' Courage from faith.
options: Fast for three days and nights

content: When Esther approached the king uninvited, what did the king do?
explanation: Esther 5:2 — The king extended the golden scepter = acceptance and grace. God protected Esther.
options: Held out the golden scepter for her to touch (acceptance)

content: On the night before Haman planned to hang Mordecai, what 'coincidence' occurred?
explanation: Esther 6:1-2 — God orchestrated 'coincidental' events to protect His people. The word 'God' is absent in the book, yet His providence is everywhere.
options: The king couldn't sleep and read the book of records, finding Mordecai had not been rewarded

content: At the banquet, what did Esther ask of the king?
explanation: Esther 7:3-4 — Esther revealed her Jewish identity and accused Haman. Her courage saved a whole people.
options: To save her life and her people

content: On what was Haman hanged?
explanation: Esther 7:10 — Poetic justice: Haman was hanged on the very gallows he had prepared for Mordecai. Whoever digs a pit will fall into it.
options: The very gallows he had prepared for Mordecai

content: Unable to revoke Haman's edict, what did the king allow the Jews to do?
explanation: Esther 8:11 — Persian law could not be revoked, but the king issued a new decree allowing the Jews to defend themselves.
options: Defend themselves and resist any who attacked them

content: By the end of the book, what did Mordecai become?
explanation: Esther 10:3 — From a man threatened with hanging to second in rank to the king. God reverses the fortunes of His people.
options: Second only to the king (prime minister)

content: How many provinces did King Ahasuerus rule, according to Esther 1:1?
explanation: Esther 1:1 — 'Now in the days of Ahasuerus, the Ahasuerus who reigned from India to Ethiopia over 127 provinces.'

content: Mordecai was of which people, and from where?
explanation: Esther 2:5-6 — 'Now there was a Jew in Susa the citadel whose name was Mordecai... who had been carried away from Jerusalem along with Jeconiah, king of Judah.'

content: Whose plot to kill the king did Mordecai uncover?
explanation: Esther 2:21 — 'Bigthan and Teresh, two of the king's eunuchs... became angry and sought to lay hands on King Ahasuerus.'

content: How many talents of silver did Haman pledge to the king to destroy the Jews?
explanation: Esther 3:9 — 'I will pay 10,000 talents of silver into the hands of those who have charge of the king's business.'

content: How tall a gallows did Haman's wife and friends advise him to build to hang Mordecai?
explanation: Esther 5:14 — 'Let a gallows fifty cubits high be made.'

content: On the 13th day of Adar — the day Haman planned destruction — what was the outcome for the Jews?
explanation: Esther 9:1 — 'On the very day when the enemies of the Jews hoped to gain the mastery over them, the reverse occurred: the Jews gained mastery over those who hated them.'
options: The Jews triumphed over their enemies

content: Mordecai wrote letters commanding the Jews to keep what annual feast to commemorate the deliverance?
explanation: Esther 9:26 — 'Therefore they called these days Purim, after the term Pur.'
